title: There’s a Global Network of Fungi Under Your Feet. This Is the First Complete Map
gist: A Science-published study produced the first global map of the arbuscular mycorrhizal fungal network, estimating it spans 110 quadrillion km, moves 4 billion tons of CO2 into soil yearly (~11% of human emissions), and is being degraded by agriculture converting grasslands four times faster than forests.

insights.claim: The global mycorrhizal fungal network is estimated to span roughly 110 quadrillion kilometers in total, nearly 1 billion times the Earth-to-sun distance, per a study published in Science.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Quantifies a previously unmeasured planetary-scale biological infrastructure that underpins soil-carbon and agriculture theses.
insights.quote: They estimate that the network stretches for roughly 110 quadrillion kilometers in total, nearly 1 billion times the distance between the Earth and the sun.
insights.locator: Beneath the Earth's surface lies an extraordinary

insights.claim: The underground fungal network is estimated to contain about 300 megatons of carbon in biomass, roughly four to six times the total mass of all living humans.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Sizes fungal biomass as a material, underappreciated component of the terrestrial carbon stock.
insights.quote: they calculate that it contains about 300 megatons of carbon in biomass—equivalent to roughly four to six times the total mass of all living humans.

insights.claim: A single teaspoon of soil can contain up to 10 meters of mycorrhizal network, illustrating the extreme density of the system.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
entities: Society for the Protection of Underground Networks
insights.so_what: Concrete density metric useful for framing soil-health and regenerative-agriculture narratives.
insights.quote: A single teaspoon of soil can contain up to 10 meters of mycorrhizal network.

insights.claim: Mycorrhizal fungal networks transport the equivalent of around 4 billion metric tons of CO2 into the soil each year, approximately 11 percent of annual human-caused CO2 emissions.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Positions soil fungal networks as a natural carbon-drawdown flow rivaling engineered carbon capture, relevant to nature-based carbon markets.
insights.quote: these fungal networks transport the equivalent of around 4 billion metric tons of carbon dioxide into the soil each year, representing approximately 11 percent of annual human-caused carbon dioxide emissions.

insights.claim: About 70 percent of all plant species depend on mycorrhizal partnerships for their survival, making the fungal network foundational to plant life globally.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Establishes systemic dependence of agriculture and ecosystems on fungi, raising the stakes of network degradation.
insights.quote: Current estimates suggest that about 70 percent of all plant species depend on these mycorrhizal partnerships for their survival.

insights.claim: Arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi operate as a symbiosis, supplying plants with water and nutrients through hyphae in exchange for carbon produced via photosynthesis.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: The exchange mechanism explains how fungi both support crop yields and sequester carbon, the dual value proposition for agtech.

insights.claim: The first global fungal map was built by compiling data from 322 previous studies plus 16,000 soil samples and applying machine learning and advanced imaging to estimate the network's extent and biomass.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Shows ML and high-resolution imaging unlocking a new environmental data layer, a template for other natural-capital measurement plays.
insights.quote: the authors of the new study compiled data from 322 previous studies, along with 16,000 soil samples collected from a wide range of terrestrial ecosystems.

insights.claim: Advances in high-resolution imaging, machine learning, and robotics are beginning to reveal previously hidden fungal network structures and how they transport nutrients and regulate climate.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Signals an emerging measurement frontier where compute-enabled tools open the subsurface biosphere to quantification and potential monetization.
insights.quote: With the advent of new technologies in high-resolution imaging, machine learning, and robotics, we are beginning to reveal what has long remained hidden beneath our feet.

insights.claim: No prior research had quantified the density and worldwide distribution of the subterranean fungal network, even though a 2025 Nature study examined global patterns in its diversity.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Confirms this is a genuinely new baseline dataset, giving first-mover framing to fungal-network science.
insights.quote: no previous research had quantified the density and worldwide distribution of this subterranean network.

insights.claim: The density of underground fungal networks in agricultural soils is only about half that found in natural ecosystems, indicating farming materially degrades the network.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Quantifies the soil-carbon cost of conventional agriculture, supporting regenerative-farming and soil-restoration investment cases.
insights.quote: the density of underground fungal networks in agricultural soils is only about half that found in natural ecosystems.

insights.claim: Grasslands hold an estimated 40 percent of the world's arbuscular mycorrhizal biomass yet are among the least protected ecosystems and are being converted to farmland four times faster than forests.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Flags grasslands, not forests, as the overlooked high-value carbon asset being lost fastest, a mispriced conservation-and-offset opportunity.
insights.quote: grasslands—which contain an estimated 40 percent of the world's arbuscular mycorrhizal biomass—are among the least protected ecosystems and are being converted to agricultural land at a rate four times faster than forests.

insights.claim: Scientists warn that less dense fungal networks could reduce the soil's capacity to store carbon and recycle nutrients.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Links network loss to a feedback that weakens a major carbon sink and crop-nutrient cycling, a systemic climate and food-security risk.
insights.quote: less dense fungal networks could reduce the soil's capacity to store carbon and recycle nutrients.

insights.claim: Understanding the fungal network points to ways of working more effectively with fungi to address challenges from food security to climate change.
insights.speaker: Marta Musso
insights.so_what: Frames fungi as an actionable lever for food and climate outcomes, the pitch underlying fungal-agtech and soil-carbon ventures.
insights.quote: it points to ways we can work more effectively with fungi to address many of the defining challenges of our time, from food security to climate change.

top_entities: Society for the Protection of Underground Networks
